audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for centurion servant, widow's son at Nain, John question, Jesus' witness to John, wisdom/children saying, and anointing woman with release/love/trust.

verses.restored: After he completed all his words in the hearing of the people, he entered Capernaum.
A certain centurion's servant, who was precious to him, was sick and about to die.
Hearing about Jesus, he sent elders of the Judeans to him, asking him to come and save/heal his servant.
Coming to Jesus, they appealed earnestly to him, saying, He is worthy for you to grant this,
for he loves our nation and built the synagogue for us.
Jesus went with them. Already not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying, Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not sufficient that you should enter under my roof.
Therefore I did not consider myself worthy to come to you; but say the word, and let my servant be healed.
For I also am a person set under authority, having soldiers under me; I say to this one, Go, and he goes, and to another, Come, and he comes, and to my servant, Do this, and he does it.
Hearing these things, Jesus marveled at him, and turning to the crowd following him, said, I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such trust.
Those who had been sent returned to the house and found the servant healthy.
Soon afterward he went to a city called Nain, and his disciples and a great crowd went with him.
As he drew near the gate of the city, look, one who had died was being carried out, an only son of his mother; she was a widow, and a considerable crowd from the city was with her.
Seeing her, the Lord was moved with compassion for her and said to her, Do not weep.
Coming near, he touched the bier, and those carrying it stood still. He said, Young man, I say to you, rise.
The dead one sat up and began to speak, and Jesus gave him to his mother.
Fear seized all, and they glorified the Divine, saying, A great prophet has been raised among us, and the Divine has visited his people.
This word about him went out in all Judea and all the surrounding region.
John's disciples reported to him concerning all these things.
Calling two of his disciples, John sent them to the Lord, saying, Are you the coming one, or should we expect another?
The men came to him and said, John the Baptist sent us to you, saying, Are you the coming one, or should we expect another?
In that hour he healed many from diseases, afflictions, and harmful spirits, and gifted sight to many blind people.

Answering, he said to them, Go report to John what you saw and heard: blind people see again, lame people walk, those with skin disease are cleansed, deaf people hear, dead people are raised, poor people receive good news.
Blessed/flourishing is whoever is not caused to stumble in me.
When John's messengers had gone away, he began to speak to the crowds concerning John: What did you go out into the wilderness to behold? A reed shaken by wind?
But what did you go out to see? A person clothed in soft garments? Look, those in glorious clothing and luxury are in royal palaces.
But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and more than a prophet.
This is the one about whom it has been written: Look, I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way before you.
I say to you, among those born of women, no one is greater than John; yet the smaller one in the reign of the Divine is greater than he.
All the people hearing, and the tax collectors, justified the Divine, having been baptized with John's baptism.
But the Pharisees and lawyers rejected the counsel of the Divine for themselves, not having been baptized by him.
To what then shall I compare the people of this generation, and what are they like?
They are like children sitting in a marketplace and calling to one another: We piped to you, and you did not dance; we lamented, and you did not weep.
For John the Baptist has come neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, He has an unclean spirit.
The Son of Humanity has come eating and drinking, and you say, Look, a glutton and wine-drinker, friend of tax collectors and sinners/misaligned ones.
Yet wisdom is justified by all her children.
One of the Pharisees asked him to eat with him. Entering the Pharisee's house, he reclined.
Look, a woman in the city, who was a sinner/misaligned woman, learning that he reclined in the Pharisee's house, brought an alabaster jar of ointment.
Standing behind at his feet, weeping, she began to wet his feet with tears, wiped them with the hair of her head, kissed his feet, and anointed them with the ointment.
Seeing this, the Pharisee who invited him said within himself, If this one were a prophet, he would know who and what sort of woman is touching him, that she is a sinner/misaligned one.
Jesus answered him, Simon, I have something to say to you. He said, Teacher, say it.
A certain lender had two debtors; one owed five hundred denarii, the other fifty.
When they had nothing to pay, he freely forgave both. Which of them, then, will love him more?
Simon answered, I suppose the one for whom he freely forgave more. He said to him, You judged rightly.
Turning toward the woman, he said to Simon, Do you see this woman? I entered your house; you gave me no water for my feet, but she wet my feet with tears and wiped them with her hair.
You gave me no kiss, but from the time I entered, she has not stopped kissing my feet.
You did not anoint my head with oil, but she anointed my feet with ointment.
For this reason I say to you: her many sins/misalignments have been released, for she loved much; but the one to whom little is released loves little.
He said to her, Your sins/misalignments are released.
Those reclining with him began to say among themselves, Who is this who even releases sins/misalignments?
He said to the woman, Your trust has saved/healed you; go into peace.
